claim: Hand A folio-level text statistics correlate with externally-observable folio features (section assignment, quire grouping, folio linear position, folio size) beyond what would be expected from a folio-to-text assignment that is random. If confirmed, the text is meaningfully linked to the manuscript's structural organisation, supporting the 'text means something' interpretation. If refuted, the text behaves as an independent overlay on the manuscript — consistent with the generative-process hypothesis being the complete story.
evidence_for: H-BV-MARKOV-VALIDATION-01 ROBUST validated the Markov-1-flat character signature manuscript-wide. If text is ALSO uncorrelated with external features, the generative-process-only interpretation becomes the strongest hypothesis. If text correlates meaningfully with external features despite Markov-flat characters, the interpretation becomes more nuanced: generative process whose output is nonetheless section-aware or context-aware.
The existing voynich-corpus.json has per-folio metadata (section, quire, folio_id, word_count, line_count, currier_language) for all 226 folios. No external catalogue fetch required.

Shuffling folio→text assignments provides a clean null distribution. If observed correlations exceed the null, the relationship is non-random. 1000 shuffles per correlation gives p-value granularity to 0.001.

8 locked correlations span categorical (section × TTR, section × length, section × outer usage, section × daiin frequency, quire × TTR) and continuous (folio position × TTR, folio position × length, word count × TTR) relationships. A mix robustly detects different kinds of structure.
evidence_against: No machine-readable illustration feature catalogue exists. Brain-V proxies illustration context via the corpus's section field (herbal/astro/bio/cosmo/pharma/recipes/zodiac/text-only). This is coarser than per-folio illustration features would be — true illustration-text correlations might be detectable at the feature level but not at the section level. False negatives are possible.

The corpus's section assignment was made by humans (cataloguers, scribes) based on illustration content. Correlations with section may reflect human categorisation rather than intrinsic text-illustration coupling.
Folio-level statistics have variable reliability: small folios (<30 tokens) produce noisy TTR and length measurements. Filtering to folios with >= 30 tokens reduces n but improves signal quality.

Multiple testing: 8 correlations at p < 0.01 each gives a ~8% chance of at least one spurious positive under no-effect null. The decision rule requires >= 3 significant to mitigate, but if exactly 1-2 are significant, the result is PARTIAL and may reflect chance.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: Hand A folios from voynich-corpus.json, filtered to folios with >= 30 tokens. This removes short label-only folios that produce noisy text statistics.

  EXTERNAL FEATURES (per folio):
    F1 Section: categorical. Values observed: {herbal, astronomical, biological, cosmological, pharmaceutical, recipes, zodiac, text-only}
    F2 Quire: categorical. Values: A, B, ..., as per corpus.
    F3 Folio linear position: continuous. Integer rank of folio in folio_id sort order.
    F4 Word count: continuous.
    F5 Line count: continuous.

  TEXT STATISTICS (per folio, Hand A only):
    T1 TTR: distinct token types / total tokens.
    T2 Mean token length: mean over tokens of len(token) in raw EVA characters.
    T3 Layer-2 outer usage: number of top-5 outers {y, n, r, ol, l} that appear in the folio (0-5).
    T4 daiin frequency: count of 'daiin' tokens / total tokens in folio.
    T5 Bigram diversity: distinct character-bigrams observed / possible_bigrams_in_alphabet_of_this_folio.

  EIGHT LOCKED CORRELATIONS:
    C1 F1 Section x T1 TTR: one-way ANOVA. Test statistic: F-stat.
    C2 F1 Section x T2 mean length: one-way ANOVA.
    C3 F1 Section x T3 Layer-2 outer usage: one-way ANOVA.
    C4 F1 Section x T4 daiin frequency: one-way ANOVA.
    C5 F2 Quire x T1 TTR: one-way ANOVA.
    C6 F3 Folio position x T1 TTR: Spearman rho.
    C7 F3 Folio position x T2 mean length: Spearman rho.
    C8 F4 Word count x T1 TTR: Spearman rho.

  NULL DISTRIBUTION:
    For each correlation Ci, generate 1000 shuffled null samples:
      Shuffle the assignment of folio_text to folio_metadata (random permutation of one column).
      Recompute the test statistic on the shuffled data.
    p-value(Ci) = fraction of null samples with test_statistic >= observed_test_statistic.

  SIGNIFICANCE:
    Significant at observed p-value < 0.01 (uncorrected).
    Record whether Bonferroni-corrected p (< 0.00125) is also met as a stricter check.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: >= 3 correlations significant at p < 0.01 uncorrected. Text is meaningfully linked to external features.
    PARTIAL: 1-2 correlations significant. Some structure detected but not robust.
    REFUTED: 0 correlations significant. Text appears independent of external folio features.

    SUBSIDIARY: count how many pass Bonferroni-corrected p < 0.00125. If >= 2, label the CONFIRMED result as ROBUSTLY_CONFIRMED.

  WORKED EXAMPLE (C1 Section x TTR):
    Group Hand A folios by section. For each section with >= 3 folios, compute TTR per folio.
    ANOVA tests whether the mean TTR differs significantly across section groups.
    Observed F-statistic: F_obs.
    Null: shuffle the section labels across folios 1000 times; recompute F each time.
    p-value = (number of null F >= F_obs) / 1000.

  REPORTING:
    For each of 8 correlations: observed test statistic, p-value (uncorrected), pass/fail at 0.01 threshold, pass/fail at 0.00125 (Bonferroni), direction of effect (which group is higher / which way correlation runs).
    Aggregate: count of significant correlations.
    Narrative interpretation for each significant result.
test_metric: 8 correlations between external features and text statistics on Hand A folios (n filtered to >=30 tokens). Null distribution from 1000 shuffles per correlation.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if >=3 sig at p<0.01 uncorrected. PARTIAL if 1-2. REFUTED if 0. ROBUSTLY_CONFIRMED subsidiary if >=2 also pass Bonferroni p<0.00125.
tests_remaining: locked_external_correlation_test: compute 8 correlations on Hand A folios with 1000 shuffle nulls; apply decision rule
reasoning: The Markov-1-flat finding established that Hand A's character-level structure is consistent with a generative process. A pure generative process whose output is independent of the manuscript's structural context would show no correlation between text statistics and external folio features. A generative process whose output is somehow parameterised or seeded by folio context would show such correlations. This hypothesis distinguishes the two possibilities. Given that no machine-readable illustration catalogue is available, the test uses the corpus's existing section and quire metadata as coarse proxies for illustration context. False negatives are possible at the finer-grained illustration-feature level, but meaningful correlations at the section level would already constitute significant evidence. The shuffled null design controls for multiple-testing baseline and sample-size effects. Methodology lesson 3 (external baseline sanity check) applied: the observed correlations are compared against a shuffle null computed on the same data rather than against external published baselines.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed test on text-external correlation following H-BV-MARKOV-VALIDATION-01 ROBUST. Illustration catalogue fetch attempted 2026-04-17 — no machine-readable catalogue found; proxying via corpus section field.
